sections.heading: What is ViewModel?
sections.content: ViewModel is a Jetpack architecture component designed to store and manage UI-related data in a lifecycle-conscious way. The ViewModel class is specifically engineered to survive configuration changes such as screen rotations, keyboard availability changes, and multi-window mode switches. Before ViewModel, developers had to save data in onSaveInstanceState() and restore it in onCreate(), which was limited to small, serializable data. ViewModel eliminates this complexity by keeping data in memory across configuration changes without any manual save-and-restore cycle.

sections.heading: Why ViewModel?
items: Survives configuration changes — data is retained across screen rotation without re-fetching from network or database
No UI references — ViewModel never holds a reference to Activity, Fragment, or View, preventing memory leaks
Lifecycle-scoped — automatically cleared when the Activity or Fragment is permanently finished (not rotated)
Clean separation — UI logic lives in the View, data management lives in the ViewModel
Shared between Fragments — a ViewModel scoped to an Activity can be shared between sibling Fragments
Works with coroutines — viewModelScope provides a coroutine scope that is automatically cancelled on ViewModel clear
Testable — ViewModel has no Android UI dependencies and can be tested with JUnit on the JVM
Integrates with SavedStateHandle for surviving process death in addition to configuration changes

rows.viewmodel_state: ViewModel is instantiated for the first time
ViewModel survives — same instance is returned to the recreated Activity
Activity gets the same ViewModel instance via by viewModels() delegate
ViewModel.onCleared() is called — ViewModel is destroyed and garbage collected
ViewModel is destroyed — use SavedStateHandle to persist critical data
ViewModel survives — Fragment's View is destroyed but ViewModel remains
ViewModel.onCleared() is called and ViewModel is destroyed

sections.heading: Creating and Using a ViewModel
sections.content: ViewModels should never be instantiated directly with a constructor. Instead, use the Jetpack-provided property delegates: by viewModels() in a Fragment or Activity, and by activityViewModels() to get a ViewModel scoped to the host Activity from inside a Fragment. These delegates ensure the correct ViewModel instance is always returned — the existing instance after a rotation, or a new instance on first creation.

sections.heading: ViewModel Instantiation Patterns
items: by viewModels() — in an Activity or Fragment; ViewModel is scoped to that Activity or Fragment
by activityViewModels() — in a Fragment; ViewModel is scoped to the host Activity and shared across all Fragments
ViewModelProvider(this).get(MyViewModel::class.java) — legacy way without delegate (avoid in new code)
by viewModels { MyViewModelFactory(repository) } — with a custom factory when ViewModel needs constructor params
With Hilt: @HiltViewModel annotation + @Inject constructor — factory is generated automatically
viewModel.onCleared() is called when the ViewModel is about to be destroyed — override it to release resources
Never call ViewModel() constructor directly in production code — always use delegates or ViewModelProvider

rows.viewmodels: Scoped to the Fragment or Activity that creates it
rows.activityviewmodels: Scoped to the host Activity — shared across all Fragments in that Activity

rows.viewmodels: Cleared when the Fragment is permanently destroyed
rows.activityviewmodels: Cleared when the Activity is permanently finished

rows.viewmodels: Not shared — each Fragment gets its own ViewModel instance
rows.activityviewmodels: Shared — all Fragments using activityViewModels() get the same instance

rows.viewmodels: Fragment-specific UI state (e.g., HomeViewModel for HomeFragment)
rows.activityviewmodels: Shared data between sibling Fragments (e.g., a cart shared between product list and checkout)

sections.heading: What is LiveData?
sections.content: LiveData is a lifecycle-aware observable data holder class from the Jetpack library. It wraps your data and allows observers (typically Activities or Fragments) to subscribe to changes. What makes LiveData special is its lifecycle awareness — it automatically manages subscription based on the observer's lifecycle state. LiveData only delivers updates to observers that are in the STARTED or RESUMED state, and it automatically removes observers when their lifecycle owner is destroyed, completely eliminating the risk of crashes from updating a destroyed UI component.

sections.heading: LiveData Key Characteristics
items: Lifecycle-aware — only delivers data to observers in STARTED or RESUMED lifecycle state
Auto-cleanup — observers are automatically removed when the lifecycle owner (Activity/Fragment) is destroyed
No crashes — never updates a stopped or destroyed UI, eliminating common IllegalStateException crashes
Always holds the latest value — new observers immediately receive the current value upon subscription
MutableLiveData is the writable subclass used inside ViewModel; expose as LiveData (read-only) to the View
Can be transformed using map() and switchMap() to derive new LiveData from existing ones
Thread-safe — postValue() can be called from background threads; setValue() must be called from the main thread
MediatorLiveData merges multiple LiveData sources into a single observable

rows.mutablelivedata: Yes — via setValue() (main thread) or postValue() (any thread)
rows.livedata: No — read-only; value can only be observed

rows.mutablelivedata: Inside ViewModel as private backing property
rows.livedata: Exposed publicly from ViewModel to the View

rows.mutablelivedata: Internal — keeps write access restricted to the ViewModel
rows.livedata: External — View can observe but cannot modify the data

sections.heading: Observing LiveData in the View
items: Call observe(lifecycleOwner, observer) on the LiveData object from the View
In Activity: viewModel.users.observe(this) { users -> updateUI(users) }
In Fragment: viewModel.users.observe(viewLifecycleOwner) { users -> updateUI(users) }
Always use viewLifecycleOwner (not 'this') in Fragments — prevents stale observers after back stack navigation
The observer lambda is called immediately with the current value when first subscribed
The observer is called again every time the LiveData value changes
observeForever() observes without a lifecycle owner — must be removed manually with removeObserver()
Use observeForever() only in non-lifecycle-aware contexts (e.g., unit tests or Application class)

rows.setvalue: Must be called on the main thread only
rows.postvalue: Can be called from any thread including background threads
Posts update to the main thread queue — may be slightly delayed

rows.setvalue: Each call triggers an observer update
rows.postvalue: Only the last value is dispatched if called multiple times rapidly before dispatch

rows.setvalue: Updating LiveData from ViewModel on main thread
rows.postvalue: Updating LiveData from a coroutine or background thread

sections.heading: LiveData Transformations
sections.content: Jetpack provides the Transformations class with two key operators — map() and switchMap() — for deriving new LiveData instances from existing ones without duplicating data. These transformations are lazy — they only compute when there is an active observer. map() transforms the value emitted by a LiveData into a new type. switchMap() is used when the transformation itself returns a LiveData, enabling dynamic LiveData sources such as database queries that depend on a search query LiveData.

sections.heading: LiveData Transformation Operators
items: Transformations.map(source) { value -> transform(value) } — transforms emitted values from one type to another
Example: val userNames = Transformations.map(users) { userList -> userList.map { it.name } }
Transformations.switchMap(source) { value -> getLiveDataFor(value) } — switches to a new LiveData based on the source value
Example: val results = Transformations.switchMap(searchQuery) { query -> repository.search(query) }
In Kotlin, use the extension functions: liveData.map { } and liveData.switchMap { } for cleaner syntax
MediatorLiveData merges multiple LiveData sources: addSource(liveData) { value -> setValue(transform(value)) }
Transformations are computed only when there is an active observer — they are lazy and efficient
Chaining transformations is supported: liveDataA.map { }.switchMap { } chains multiple operations

rows.description: A CoroutineScope tied to the ViewModel's lifecycle — automatically cancelled when ViewModel is cleared
viewModelScope.launch { /* coroutine work */ } — launches a coroutine inside the ViewModel
Runs on Dispatchers.Main by default — switch to Dispatchers.IO for network or database operations
All coroutines launched in viewModelScope are cancelled automatically when onCleared() is called
viewModelScope.launch { _users.value = repository.getUsers() } — fetches and updates LiveData

sections.heading: SavedStateHandle — Surviving Process Death
items: SavedStateHandle is a key-value map that survives both configuration changes AND process death
Inject it into the ViewModel constructor — Jetpack provides it automatically via SavedStateViewModelFactory
With Hilt, SavedStateHandle is injected automatically into @HiltViewModel ViewModels
Read values: val userId = savedStateHandle.get<Int>("userId")
Write values: savedStateHandle["userId"] = userId
Get as LiveData: val userIdLiveData = savedStateHandle.getLiveData<Int>("userId")
Get as StateFlow: val userIdFlow = savedStateHandle.getStateFlow("userId", defaultValue)
Only use SavedStateHandle for small, critical state — for large data use Room database persistence

rows.viewmodel: No practical limit — held in memory
Any Kotlin object, lists, complex data
Large data, network results, database queries
rows.onsaveinstancestate: Small UI state like scroll position, selected tab, input text
Use for lightweight UI state not already in ViewModel

sections.heading: Sharing ViewModel Between Fragments
items: Use by activityViewModels() in each Fragment that needs to share data
The ViewModel is scoped to the Activity — all Fragments in that Activity get the same instance
Fragment A updates the ViewModel: viewModel.selectUser(user)
Fragment B observes the same ViewModel LiveData and reacts to the selection automatically
This is the recommended pattern for master-detail UIs (list Fragment + detail Fragment)
Also use shared ViewModel for multi-step form flows where data accumulates across screens
With Jetpack Navigation Component, use by navGraphViewModels(R.id.nav_graph) to scope ViewModel to a navigation subgraph
Scoping to a nav graph destroys the ViewModel when the user exits the subgraph — cleaner than Activity scope

rows.code_description: class UserViewModel(private val repo: UserRepository) : ViewModel() { private val _users = MutableLiveData<List<User>>(); val users: LiveData<List<User>> = _users }
private val viewModel: UserViewModel by viewModels()
viewModel.loadUsers() called in onViewCreated() or init block of ViewModel

sections.heading: Common ViewModel & LiveData Mistakes
items: Storing Activity or View reference in ViewModel — causes memory leaks; use Application context via AndroidViewModel if context is needed
Using 'this' instead of viewLifecycleOwner in Fragment observers — causes multiple observer registrations
Calling setValue() from a background thread — use postValue() for background thread updates
Exposing MutableLiveData publicly from ViewModel — always expose as LiveData to enforce encapsulation
Creating ViewModel with new keyword — always use by viewModels() or ViewModelProvider
Not cancelling background work in onCleared() — use viewModelScope which auto-cancels on ViewModel clear
Re-triggering data loads in onViewCreated() on every Fragment recreation — check if data is already loaded first
Using LiveData for one-time navigation events without SingleLiveEvent — causes re-navigation on rotation

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: ViewModel and LiveData are the twin pillars of Android's recommended architecture. ViewModel provides a lifecycle-aware container that survives configuration changes and keeps data management separate from UI concerns. LiveData provides a safe, lifecycle-aware communication channel from the ViewModel back to the View, ensuring the UI is always up to date without risking crashes from updating destroyed components. Together with viewModelScope for coroutines and SavedStateHandle for process death survival, they form a complete, robust solution for managing UI state in any Android app.
